detection.semanticDescription:
This pull request should
TRA-59 Implement GeminiModelAdapter

**Context** The runner, environment, and trace pipeline all work today, but the only agent "brain" is the deterministic fixture script. `GeminiModelAdapter.next_action` is currently a scaffold that raises `NotImplementedError` ([src/trace_harness/models/gemini.py]()). Implementing the reviewed implementation turns the scripted skeleton into a real agent — the first time a live model actually drives the refund/support loop. the reviewed implementation is the top "Build next" item for the `models/` module. **Objective** Implement `GeminiModelAdapter.next_action` so a real Gemini model can drive a task end-to-end, returning the same normalized `AgentAction` the fixture adapter produces — so the runner, verifiers, attribution, and dashboard never know which provider produced a trace. **Scope** Transcript → Gemini contents conversion; `ToolSpec` → Gemini function declarations (native tool mode); the `google-genai` SDK call; response normalization into one `AgentAction` (tool call or final answer) with the raw payload stashed in `AgentAction.raw`; SDK-error mapping to `ModelAdapterError`. Native function-calling first; JSON tool-mode fallback can follow. **Deliverables** * `next_action` implemented per the 5-step design in the module docstring. * `_transcript_to_contents(transcript)` and `_tools_to_declarations(tools)` helpers (reading structured `tool_call` data from `Message.metadata`, per [TRA-58](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-58/agent-skeleton-fixes-transcript-shape-model-call-timeout-cli-provider) — not re-parsing JSON from `content`). * Response → `AgentAction` normalization, with `raw` populated. * SDK errors mapped to `ModelAdapterError` so the runner records a clean `model_error` termination. * Offline unit tests using a **recorded** SDK response (no live API call). * A `model_response` trace event emitted by the runner so the raw payload lands in the trace (today the runner records `model_action` with `raw` excluded and never emits `model_response`). * README/.env note for obtaining a free Gemini key. **Acceptance Criteria** * A task runs end-to-end with `--provider gemini` (CLI plumbing landed in [TRA-58](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-58/agent-skeleton-fixes-transcript-shape-model-call-timeout-cli-provider)). * Tool calls and final answers are normalized identically to the fixture adapter (same downstream artifacts). * SDK failures terminate the run as `model_error`, not an uncaught crash. * **Tests stay fully offline** — no test requires an API key or network (repo-wide constraint). * The trace records the provider's raw response payload. **Suggested Technical Approach** Start with `tool_mode = native` function calling. Build the transcript→contents converter against a recorded response and unit-test the normalization in isolation before any live call. Keep the live request behind a per-call request timeout so a hung call is bounded at the provider level (complements the runner-level daemon-thread timeout from [TRA-58](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-58/agent-skeleton-fixes-transcript-shape-model-call-timeout-cli-provider)). **Collaborators / Reviewers** Samrath (trace — `model_response` event + raw payload shape), Karan Gupta / Darrel Wihandi (confirm real-model traces carry enough for verification/attribution, especially when `reasoning` is absent). **Dependencies** Runner skeleton ([TRA-7](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-7/build-baseline-target-agent-runner-skeleton)), CLI provider flag + model-call timeout ([TRA-58](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-58/agent-skeleton-fixes-transcript-shape-model-call-timeout-cli-provider)), the `models/base.py` contract types, and `google-genai` (already an optional `[gemini]` dependency). **Handoff / Blocks** Unblocks real (non-scripted) agent runs, multi-run orchestration with non-deterministic models, and any evaluation that needs live-model behavior. **Out of Scope** Retries / backoff, rate limiting, cost tracking, parallel tool calls, streaming, and providers other than Gemini.

TRA-67 Define live-agent readiness criteria for Gemini and non-fixture evaluation runs

## Context TRACE's `main` branch has a deterministic fixture-based vertical slice. That is valuable, but the next product question is when a real model/provider run should count as a meaningful TRACE evaluation instead of just an API smoke test. Open Gemini work (`TRA-59`, PR [trace-watai/trace#64](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/implement-geminimodeladapternext-action-first-live-provider-c0f67230ba5a)) is the first live-adapter step. the reviewed implementation depends on runner transcript shape, model-call timeout, provider flags, trace events, environment behavior, verifier readiness, artifact read paths, and controlled RAG/tool behavior. Without explicit readiness criteria, the team can easily merge a live adapter that technically calls a model but does not produce enough evidence for verification, attribution, failure bundles, regression, or dashboard replay. PR [trace-watai/trace#70](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-23-mock-environment-validation-and-unit-tests-7dfe53cb1cbc) (`TRA-23`) and PR [trace-watai/trace#71](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-24-pinned-document-retrieval-tests-9cc9aaf4ae04) (`TRA-24`) are part of live readiness too: a Gemini run against unstable tools or uncontrolled retrieval is only an API smoke test, not a TRACE-ready evaluation. ## Objective Define the readiness criteria for live Gemini and future non-fixture evaluation runs. Justin should give the team a clear decision framework for when a live run is TRACE-ready, demo-ready, or still just experimental. ## Scope Cover the full chain from provider call to downstream artifacts: * Model adapter behavior and normalized `AgentAction` output. * Transcript shape and provider metadata. * Tool-call normalization, final answer normalization, error handling, timeout behavior, and retry boundaries. * Controlled support environment behavior, deterministic tool side effects, and inspectable final state. * Controlled retrieval behavior, stable provenance, and ranking overrides needed for reproducible failures. * `model_response` trace event expectations and what raw provider payload may safely contain. * Offline test policy versus manual live smoke test policy. * Required run artifacts for verifier, attribution, failure bundle, regression, and dashboard consumers. * What needs to be true before a Gemini run can be used in the demo narrative or readiness review. ## Deliverables * Live-agent readiness checklist with three levels: * Experimental: allowed for local exploration only. * Integration-ready: produces artifacts downstream systems can consume. * Demo-ready: stable enough to show as part of the TRACE product story. * Merge/readiness questions for Rupert, Samrath, Karan, Darrel, Skye, Evan Yang, and Emily. * Recommended manual smoke command and expected artifacts for a real Gemini run. * Risk note for API keys, rate limits, provider timeouts, raw payload redaction, non-determinism, environment nondeterminism, retrieval ranking drift, and no-network CI rules. * Decision recommendation on whether the first demo should remain fixture-first, live-model optional, or live-model required. ## Acceptance Criteria * Criteria distinguish "provider call works" from "TRACE evaluation works." * Every readiness criterion maps to a downstream consumer or product claim. * The checklist explicitly preserves offline tests and fixture determinism. * The checklist names the minimal manual live validation needed before demo use. * The checklist accounts for PR [trace-watai/trace#64](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/implement-geminimodeladapternext-action-first-live-provider-c0f67230ba5a), PR [trace-watai/trace#70](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-23-mock-environment-validation-and-unit-tests-7dfe53cb1cbc), PR [trace-watai/trace#71](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-24-pinned-document-retrieval-tests-9cc9aaf4ae04), PR [trace-watai/trace#79](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-20-run-data-read-apis-via-a-typed-runreader-service-23192dfc8fdd), and PR [trace-watai/trace#80](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-54-recovery-re-land-orphaned-run-index-pr-56-75f450b-ad22c0819d22) because live runs depend on model behavior, environment/RAG behavior, and artifact-read behavior together. * Open questions are assigned to the correct owners and linked back to Linear issues. * Katharine has at least one technical audit follow-up if code-level contract validation is needed. ## Suggested Technical Approach Start from these current sources: * `src/trace_harness/models/gemini.py` on `main` still scaffolds `next_action` as `NotImplementedError`. * PR [trace-watai/trace#64](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/implement-geminimodeladapternext-action-first-live-provider-c0f67230ba5a) implements native Gemini function calling, `model_response` events, and offline adapter tests. * PR [trace-watai/trace#70](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-23-mock-environment-validation-and-unit-tests-7dfe53cb1cbc) validates mock environment/tool/state/retrieval behavior. * PR [trace-watai/trace#71](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-24-pinned-document-retrieval-tests-9cc9aaf4ae04) adds pinned retrieval/ranking controls. * PR [trace-watai/trace#79](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-20-run-data-read-apis-via-a-typed-runreader-service-23192dfc8fdd) and PR [trace-watai/trace#80](https://linear.app/trace-watai/review/tra-54-recovery-re-land-orphaned-run-index-pr-56-75f450b-ad22c0819d22) define the read path and run-index recovery that live runs will need for dashboard/evaluation consumers. * `src/trace_harness/runner/agent_runner.py` owns the step loop and event emission. * `docs/modules.md` defines no-network tests, schema versioning, and runner/model boundaries. * `docs/trace_schema.md` reserves `model_response` for real provider output. ## Collaborators / Reviewers * Rupert: Gemini adapter and runner behavior. * Samrath: trace/artifact contract and model_response event shape. * Evan Yang: environment/tool/RAG behavior under live model calls. * Emily: task validity and fixture readiness. * Karan and Darrel: enough evidence for verifier and attribution. * Skye: dashboard visibility of live-run artifacts. * Katharine: code-level readiness audit. * Mohammed and Sarp: demo/readiness decision. ## Dependencies No hard blocker for writing criteria. Final validation depends on the Gemini PR and any required environment, retrieval, runner, and read-path PRs landing. ## Handoff / Blocks Feeds `TRA-64`, `TRA-59`, `TRA-41`, `TRA-52`, and demo readiness planning. the reviewed implementation should make clear what the team must prove before treating live-agent output as product evidence. ## Out of Scope * Implementing Gemini adapter code. * Choosing long-term provider strategy. * Requiring live API calls in CI. * Expanding beyond the refund/support vertical slice before the first live-readiness path is validated.

reviewComment:
TRA-68 Deep audit verifier evidence, failure-card, and frontend contract alignment

## Context Verifier evidence now sits at the center of several active handoffs. Karan's verifier schema work, Samir's failure-card/repair schemas, Skye's frontend contracts, and Darrel's attribution semantics all consume the same evidence and step IDs. Current evidence from the audit: * On `main`, `EvidenceItem.kind` is still an open string and `VERIFIER_RESULT_SCHEMA_VERSION` is `0.1.0`. * PR #72 constrains `EvidenceItem.kind` to an `EvidenceKind` enum and bumps the verifier result schema. * PR #73 introduces a `VerifierInput` model. * The dashboard currently has `apps/dashboard/src/types/evidence.ts`, but its comment says `kind` should become an enum after backend values exist. * PR #74 changes failure-card/repair package schemas and needs dashboard validation. This is exactly the kind of cross-stream, code-level contract risk Katharine should own under the new role. ## Objective Perform a deep technical audit of verifier evidence, failure-card artifacts, and frontend TypeScript mirrors so the team knows exactly what must align before these contracts are considered stable. ## Scope Inspect and compare: * Backend verifier models: `VerifierResult`, `FailedCheck`, `EvidenceItem`, severity, blocks-release semantics, schema version. * Refund verifier evidence emissions and check IDs. * Failure-card and repair-package schemas plus generated artifact fields. * Frontend contracts for evidence, verifier result, failure card, attribution, severity, and trace-event consumers. * Expected fixture artifacts and parser tests. * Open PRs #72, #73, #74, #78 and linked Linear issues. ## Deliverables * Code-level compatibility table: ```text Field / enum / artifact | Backend source | Frontend/consumer source | Current main | Pending PR | Risk | Required change/test ``` * List of exact field/version mismatches, including whether each is blocking or non-blocking. * Recommended owner action for Karan, Samir, Skye, and Darrel. * Suggested validation fixture or command proving a verifier result can become a rendered dashboard failure detail without ad hoc parsing. * Comment-ready summary for the relevant Linear issues or PRs. ## Acceptance Criteria * Audit cites exact files, schema versions, PR numbers, and Linear IDs. * `EvidenceItem.kind` enum alignment is resolved or has a clearly assigned follow-up. * Failure-card schema version differences between backend and frontend are identified and assigned. * Step IDs and evidence references are checked against trace-event availability. * The audit says whether the dashboard can safely render verifier/failure-card evidence today, after pending PRs, or only after additional work. * Justin can fold the result into `TRA-64` / `TRA-66` without re-reading all code himself. ## Suggested Technical Approach Start with: * `src/trace_harness/verifiers/base.py` * `src/trace_harness/verifiers/refund_policy.py` * `src/trace_harness/failure_bundles/schemas.py` * `src/trace_harness/failure_bundles/generator.py` * `src/trace_harness/attribution/schemas.py` * `apps/dashboard/src/types/evidence.ts` * `apps/dashboard/src/types/failure-card.ts` * `apps/dashboard/src/types/attribution.ts` * frontend verifier/run-result PR #78 when available Use the repo rule from `docs/modules.md`: schema changes must bump `schema_version` and update consumers/tests in the same PR. ## reviewComment:
TRA-13 Define TRACE failure taxonomy and step semantics v1

## Context TRACE attribution must explain why a verified failure happened without overriding deterministic pass/fail. The team needs shared step semantics before judge prompts, failure cards, and dashboard markers diverge. ## Objective Define the first TRACE failure taxonomy and the exact meaning of diagnostic step markers. ## Scope Cover planning failure, query-formation failure, retrieval failure, source-precedence failure, reasoning failure, state-tracking failure, tool-selection/action failure, tool implementation failure, grounding/citation failure, context/memory leakage, and false completion. Define first suspicious anomaly, first incorrect reasoning step, missed recovery point, first unrecoverable trajectory step, first irreversible external action, visible harmful symptom, and user-facing symptom. ## Deliverables * Taxonomy v1 with definitions. * Concrete examples using trace step IDs or fixture-style steps. * Ambiguous-case notes. * Mapping to verifier evidence and failure-card fields. ## Acceptance Criteria * Definitions are mutually understandable and not circular. * Examples use concrete trace steps. * Ambiguous cases are documented. * Justin reviews alignment with related work. * Katharine can use the taxonomy for human audit. ## Suggested Technical Approach Start as a concise markdown/spec artifact and keep names stable enough for schema fields. ## Collaborators / Reviewers Justin Lam, Katharine, Samir Mohammed, Karan Gupta. ## Dependencies Can proceed alongside TaskSpec and trace schema but must align with both. ## Handoff / Blocks Blocks judge schema, dashboard diagnostic timeline semantics, attribution audits, and failure-card language. ## Out of Scope Final academic taxonomy claims and broad non-support-agent failure modes beyond Month 1.

Review in Linear

reviewComment: `rebuild_index()` will currently raise (and prevent index self-heal) if any existing `run_result.json` is malformed JSON or missing required fields for `RunIndexEntry`. Since `upsert_index_entry()` relies on `rebuild_index()` to recover from a corrupt/hand-edited index, it’s more robust to skip invalid `run_result.json` files (optionally warning) rather than failing the entire rebuild.
acceptedCode:
A runs-dir-level ``index.json`` sits alongside the run directories: one
summary entry per run for cheap listing without scanning every directory. It
is a *derived, rebuildable* convenience (see :meth:`ArtifactStore.rebuild_index`),
not a per-run artifact — so it is deliberately absent from ``ALL_ARTIFACTS``
and exempt from the per-run partial-artifacts promise.
from trace_harness.tracing.run_index import RunIndex, RunIndexEntry
# Runs-dir-level (not per-run): a derived, rebuildable index of all runs.
RUN_INDEX = "index.json"
    # --- run index ---
    def index_path(self) -> Path:
        return self.runs_dir / RUN_INDEX
    def read_index(self) -> RunIndex:

codeContext.reviewedContext:
def rebuild_index(self) -> RunIndex:
        """Reconstruct the index by scanning run dirs for ``run_result.json``.

        Runs without a result (crashed before finalize) are skipped. The result
        is written back atomically and returned.
        """
        entries: list[RunIndexEntry] = []
        for run_id in self.list_runs():
            if not self.exists(run_id, RUN_RESULT):
                continue
            entries.append(RunIndexEntry.model_validate(self.read_json(run_id, RUN_RESULT)))
        index = RunIndex(entries=sorted(entries, key=lambda e: e.run_id))
        self._write_index(index)
        return index
codeContext.acceptedContext:
One run, one directory::

    runs/{run_id}/
      task_spec.json            # what was asked (snapshot, replayable)
      run_config.json           # how it was run
      initial_state.json        # the world before
      trace.jsonl               # what happened (one TraceEvent per line)
      final_state.json          # the world after
      run_result.json           # how it ended
      verifier_result.json      # did it actually succeed (written by `verify`)
      attribution_result.json   # where/why it failed (written by `attribute`)
      failure_card.json         # human-readable failure summary (written by `bundle`)
      repair_package.json       # engineering recommendations (written by `bundle`)
      regression_artifact.json  # rerunnable regression spec (written by `bundle`)

The first six are written by the runner; the rest appear as the pipeline
stages run. Partial directories are *valid* — a crashed run keeps whatever
it managed to write, and every file is independently parseable JSON with a
``schema_version`` field.

A runs-dir-level ``index.json`` sits alongside the run directories: one
summary entry per run for cheap listing without scanning every directory. It
is a *derived, rebuildable* convenience (see :meth:`ArtifactStore.rebuild_index`),
not a per-run artifact — so it is deliberately absent from ``ALL_ARTIFACTS``
and exempt from the per-run partial-artifacts promise.

This local-JSON layout *is* the data contract the future API server and
dashboard read (see docs/future_api.md and docs/future_dashboard.md).
Renaming a file here is a breaking
change for them — coordinate.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from pydantic import BaseModel

reviewComment:
## Pull request overview Adds a runs-directory–level `index.json` to support cheap run listing without scanning every `runs/{run_id}/` directory, and wires it into the runner with guarded “best-effort” updates. **Changes:** - Introduces `RunIndex` / `RunIndexEntry` schemas for a denormalized summary of `run_result.json`. - Extends `ArtifactStore` with index read/upsert and full rebuild capabilities using atomic writes. - Updates the runner finalize path to upsert the index entry after writing `run_result.json`, and adjusts/expands tests accordingly. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 5 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | `src/trace_harness/tracing/run_index.py` | New Pydantic models for the runs-dir index format. | | `src/trace_harness/tracing/artifact_store.py` | Adds index file path/read/upsert/rebuild and atomic index writes. | | `src/trace_harness/runner/agent_runner.py` | Guarded hook to upsert index entry after `run_result.json` is written. | | `tests/test_run_index.py` | New tests covering index semantics, rebuild, and guarded runner hook behavior. | | `tests/test_cli.py` | Adjusts a CLI test to count run directories (since `index.json` is added at runs-dir root). |

reviewComment:
TRA-54 Run index file for cheap run listing

**Context:** Sub-issue of [TRA-8](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-8/implement-jsonl-logging-validation-and-artifact-storage) (storage). Realizes the `# TODO(Samrath/tracing)` in `src/trace_harness/tracing/artifact_store.py:28` — a run index file so consumers can list runs cheaply without scanning every `runs/{run_id}/` directory. **Scope (storage / write side only):** * Define a `RunIndexEntry` (Pydantic v2): `run_id`, `task_id`, `status`, verifier verdict, key timestamps, `schema_version`. * Maintain `runs/index.json` — one entry per run, written/updated atomically on run finalize via the existing `ArtifactStore.write_json` (tmp + fsync + `os.replace`, shipped in PR #54). * Reader + round-trip test; tolerate a missing/empty index (rebuildable from `runs/` directories). **Out of scope:** the read API that exposes listing — that's [TRA-20](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-20/expose-run-data-read-apis-for-dashboard-and-evaluation) (run-data read APIs), which consumes this index. Keep this PR small and single-purpose. **Why now:** pairs directly with the atomic-writes work just landed, and unblocks [TRA-20](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-20/expose-run-data-read-apis-for-dashboard-and-evaluation)'s "list runs" deliverable.

Review in Linear

reviewComment:
TRA-58 Agent skeleton fixes: transcript shape, model-call timeout, CLI provider flag

Follow-up hardening on the baseline target-agent runner ([TRA-7](https://linear.app/trace-watai/issue/TRA-7/build-baseline-target-agent-runner-skeleton)), preparing it for the first real model adapter. Three changes: **1. Transcript stores structured data in** `metadata`, not JSON-in-a-string `_action_to_assistant_message` / `_observation_to_tool_message` previously `json.dumps`'d the whole action/observation into `Message.content`, leaving `metadata` empty. A real adapter would have to `json.loads` it back out to reconstruct tool calls. Now `content` holds only human-readable text (reasoning / final answer) and the structured fields (`kind`, `tool_call`, tool result) live in `metadata`, ready for a provider to consume directly. Done now because nothing reads the transcript yet — the cost of changing this contract rises once the Gemini adapter depends on it. * *Note for tracing (Samrath):* `model_prompt` events now show populated `metadata` instead of JSON-in-`content`. No consumer reads it today; flagging the shape change. **2. Run timeout now bounds a hung model call** The timeout was only checked between steps, so a hung provider call could hang the run forever (the "enforce maximum time" criterion wasn't actually enforced). The model call now runs in a daemon thread bounded by the run's *remaining* budget; on overrun it records a `model_timeout` error event and terminates with `TerminationReason.TIMEOUT`. * *Known limitation:* Python can't kill the thread, so a truly hung call runs on in the background (as a daemon — never blocks process exit). reviewComment:
TRA-8 Implement JSONL logging, validation, and artifact storage

## Context TRACE needs durable, reloadable run artifacts so verifier, attribution, failure bundles, regression tests, and dashboard views can consume the same source of truth. ## Objective Implement JSONL trace logging, validation, and artifact storage for Month 1 runs. ## Scope Create the artifact layout: ```text runs/{run_id}/ task_spec.json agent_config.json trace.jsonl initial_state.json final_state.json verifier_result.json attribution.json failure_card.json repair_package.json regression_test.json ``` ## Deliverables * JSONL event writer and reader. * Validation on write/read. * Artifact directory creation and partial-run preservation. * Schema version fields. * Round-trip serialization tests. ## Acceptance Criteria * Partial runs are preserved after failure. * Malformed events fail validation with useful errors. * Trace files can be reloaded without loss. * Artifacts are versioned or include schema versions. * Tests cover round-trip serialization. ## Suggested Technical Approach Use Pydantic validation, deterministic run IDs/config snapshots, and simple file-backed storage before adding databases. ## Collaborators / Reviewers Rupert Maiti, Karan Gupta, Samir Mohammed. ## Dependencies Requires trace/run schema and runner skeleton event interface. ## Handoff / Blocks Blocks verifier/judge artifact storage, failure bundle generation, dashboard live-data integration, and reproducible reruns. ## Out of Scope Production object storage, distributed tracing export, and complex retention policy.

from __future__ import annotations

codeContext.acceptedContext:
"""Pydantic schemas for tasks — the structured test scenarios TRACE runs.

A *task* is the unit of evaluation: it describes the world the agent starts
in (``initial_state``), what it may use (``available_tools``,
``available_docs``), what good behavior looks like (``expected_behavior``,
``forbidden_actions``, ``required_evidence``), and which deterministic
verifiers judge the outcome (``verifier_ids``).

Design notes
    - ``extra="forbid"`` so typos in JSON fixtures fail loudly at load time
      instead of silently producing a different scenario.
    - ``initial_state`` stays a plain dict here on purpose: its shape is
      owned by the *environment* that interprets it (for the first vertical
      slice, :class:`trace_harness.environment.state.SupportState`). Typed
      validation happens when the environment parses it.
    - ``severity`` expresses how bad it is *if an agent fails this task*,
      and seeds the severity of downstream failure artifacts.

Two layers of validation (keep them separate)
    - **Structural** (here): rules every ``TaskSpec`` must satisfy, including
      minimal unit-test stubs — slug/version/workflow shapes, no duplicate
      list entries, resolvable docs. Deliberately lenient about *content*
      (empty tool/verifier lists are allowed) so other modules can build stub
      tasks in their unit tests.
    - **Authoring quality** (``tasks/validation.py`` + the task-validity
      rubric, applied to real fixtures under ``fixtures/tasks/``): the
      stricter "is this a *good* task to evaluate on" checks — non-empty
      tools/verifiers/failure-modes, describes correct behavior, no clocks in
      state, multi-step. Those do NOT live here so they cannot break stubs.

# once we settle on how multi-turn tasks represent the inbound conversation
# (touches the runner's transcript builder — coordinate with Rupert).

from enum import StrEnum

"""Pydantic schemas for tasks — the structured test scenarios TRACE runs.

A *task* is the unit of evaluation: it describes the world the agent starts
in (``initial_state``), what it may use (``available_tools``,
``available_docs``), what good behavior looks like (``expected_behavior``,
``forbidden_actions``, ``required_evidence``), and which deterministic
verifiers judge the outcome (``verifier_ids``).

    - ``extra="forbid"`` so typos in JSON fixtures fail loudly at load time
      instead of silently producing a different scenario.
    - ``initial_state`` stays a plain dict here on purpose: its shape is
      owned by the *environment* that interprets it (for the first vertical
      slice, :class:`trace_harness.environment.state.SupportState`). Typed
      validation happens when the environment parses it.
    - ``severity`` expresses how bad it is *if an agent fails this task*,
      and seeds the severity of downstream failure artifacts.

Two layers of validation (keep them separate)
    - **Structural** (here): rules every ``TaskSpec`` must satisfy, including
      minimal unit-test stubs — slug/version/workflow shapes, no duplicate
      list entries, resolvable docs. Deliberately lenient about *content*
      (empty tool/verifier lists are allowed) so other modules can build stub
      tasks in their unit tests.

      stricter "is this a *good* task to evaluate on" checks — non-empty
      tools/verifiers/failure-modes, describes correct behavior, no clocks in
      state, multi-step. Those do NOT live here so they cannot break stubs.

codeContext.acceptedContext:
"""Pydantic schemas for tasks — the structured test scenarios TRACE runs.

A *task* is the unit of evaluation: it describes the world the agent starts
in (``initial_state``), what it may use (``available_tools``,
``available_docs``), what good behavior looks like (``expected_behavior``,
``forbidden_actions``, ``required_evidence``), and which deterministic
verifiers judge the outcome (``verifier_ids``).

Design notes
    - ``extra="forbid"`` so typos in JSON fixtures fail loudly at load time
      instead of silently producing a different scenario.
    - ``initial_state`` stays a plain dict here on purpose: its shape is
      owned by the *environment* that interprets it (for the first vertical
      slice, :class:`trace_harness.environment.state.SupportState`). Typed
      validation happens when the environment parses it.
    - ``severity`` expresses how bad it is *if an agent fails this task*,
      and seeds the severity of downstream failure artifacts.

Two layers of validation (keep them separate)
    - **Structural** (here): rules every ``TaskSpec`` must satisfy, including
      minimal unit-test stubs — slug/version/workflow shapes, no duplicate
      list entries, resolvable docs. Deliberately lenient about *content*
      (empty tool/verifier lists are allowed) so other modules can build stub
      tasks in their unit tests.
    - **Authoring quality** (``tasks/validation.py`` + the task-validity
      rubric, applied to real fixtures under ``fixtures/tasks/``): the
      stricter "is this a *good* task to evaluate on" checks — non-empty
      tools/verifiers/failure-modes, describes correct behavior, no clocks in
      state, multi-step. Those do NOT live here so they cannot break stubs.

# TODO(Emily/tasks): promote ``metadata.user_message`` to a first-class field
# once we settle on how multi-turn tasks represent the inbound conversation
# (touches the runner's transcript builder — coordinate with Rupert).
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import Iterable
from enum import StrEnum
from typing import Any

        ),

    @field_validator("available_tools", "available_docs", "verifier_ids", "targeted_failure_modes")
    @classmethod
    def _no_duplicate_entries(cls, value: list[str], info: ValidationInfo) -> list[str]:
        seen: set[str] = set()
        dupes: set[str] = set()
        for entry in value:
            if entry in seen:
                dupes.add(entry)
            seen.add(entry)
        if dupes:
            raise ValueError(f"{info.field_name} contains duplicate entries: {sorted(dupes)}")
        return value

    @model_validator(mode="after")
    def _docs_must_be_resolvable(self) -> TaskSpec:
        """If a task names doc ids, they must be resolvable — either via a
        ``docs_fixture`` or inline ``initial_state['docs']`` — so the loader
        never silently drops referenced docs."""
        if self.available_docs and self.docs_fixture is None and "docs" not in self.initial_state:
            raise ValueError(
                "available_docs is set but there is no docs_fixture and no inline "
                "initial_state['docs']; the referenced doc ids cannot be resolved"
            )
        return self

"""Structural validation tests for TaskSpec.

These cover the *schema-level* rules only — the universal structural contract.
Authoring-quality rules (non-empty tools/verifiers, describes behavior, no
clocks, multi-step) live in tasks/validation.py + the rubric and are tested
separately, so minimal stub tasks (e.g. in test_refund_verifier) stay valid.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from pathlib import Path

import pytest
from pydantic import ValidationError

from trace_harness.tasks.loader import load_task
from trace_harness.tasks.schemas import TaskSpec

REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
TASKS_DIR = REPO_ROOT / "fixtures" / "tasks"
VALID_TASK_PATH = TASKS_DIR / "refund_policy_valid_cash.json"
FAILURE_TASK_PATH = TASKS_DIR / "refund_policy_failure.json"


def _valid_kwargs() -> dict:
    """Minimal kwargs that satisfy the structural schema."""
    return {
        "task_id": "unit_task",
        "title": "unit",
        "description": "unit",
        "goal": "unit",
        "workflow_type": "support.refund",
        "initial_state": {},
        "available_tools": ["get_order"],
    }


# --- the real fixtures must still load -------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize("path", [VALID_TASK_PATH, FAILURE_TASK_PATH])
def test_real_fixtures_load(path) -> None:
    task = load_task(path)
    assert task.task_id


# --- extra="forbid" --------------------------------------------------------


def test_unknown_field_is_rejected() -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="Extra inputs"):
        TaskSpec(**_valid_kwargs(), typo_field=1)


# --- required text fields reject empty (min_length) ------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize("field", ["title", "description", "goal"])
def test_required_text_fields_reject_empty(field: str) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="at least 1 character"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), field: ""})


# --- task_id slug pattern --------------------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_id", ["Refund_Task", "refund task", "-leading", "réfund"])
def test_task_id_must_be_a_slug(bad_id: str) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="should match pattern"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "task_id": bad_id})


# --- workflow_type pattern -------------------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_wf", ["Support.Refund", "support refund", "support."])
def test_workflow_type_must_be_dotted_lowercase(bad_wf: str) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="should match pattern"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "workflow_type": bad_wf})


# --- schema_version semver -------------------------------------------------


def test_schema_version_must_be_semver() -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="should match pattern"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "schema_version": "v1"})


# --- uniqueness (all four list fields) -------------------------------------


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "field", ["available_tools", "available_docs", "verifier_ids", "targeted_failure_modes"]
)
def test_duplicate_list_entries_rejected(field: str) -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="duplicate entries"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), field: ["dup", "dup"]})


# --- difficulty (optional, enum-validated) ---------------------------------


def test_difficulty_is_optional_and_validated() -> None:
    assert TaskSpec(**_valid_kwargs()).difficulty is None
    assert TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "difficulty": "hard"}).difficulty == "hard"
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="Input should be"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "difficulty": "trivial"})


# --- docs resolvability ----------------------------------------------------


def test_available_docs_without_source_is_rejected() -> None:
    with pytest.raises(ValidationError, match="cannot be resolved"):
        TaskSpec(**{**_valid_kwargs(), "available_docs": ["policy_v4"]})


def test_available_docs_with_inline_docs_ok() -> None:
    task = TaskSpec(
        **{
            **_valid_kwargs(),
            "available_docs": ["policy_v4"],
            "initial_state": {"docs": [{"doc_id": "policy_v4"}]},
        }
    )
    assert task.available_docs == ["policy_v4"]


# --- leniency the schema must preserve (so stubs keep working) -------------


def test_empty_content_lists_are_structurally_valid() -> None:
    """available_tools=[] and no verifier/failure-mode lists must parse — those
    are authoring-quality concerns, enforced by validate-fixtures, not here."""

reviewComment:
TRA-53 Tighten TaskSpec definitions + structural validation

**Context:** The foundation ships a baseline `TaskSpec` in `trace_harness/tasks/schemas.py`. Own it into a fuller version, tighten loose/unclear fields without breaking sibling stubs. **Scope:** Schema-level *structural* rules only (not authoring-quality). Add per-field descriptions; patterns for `task_id` (slug), `workflow_type` (dotted-lowercase), `schema_version` (semver); non-empty `title/description/goal`; uniqueness + docs-resolvability validators; optional `difficulty` enum; bump `TASK_SCHEMA_VERSION`. Keep lenient (empty tool/verifier lists allowed). **Deliverables:** updated `schemas.py`; `tests/test_task_schema.py`; `difficulty` + `schema_version` reflected in the two task fixtures. **Cross-stream:** Darrel (`targeted_failure_modes` left as free strings, should align w/ the Judge's `failure_category` vocabulary), Rupert (`user_message` TODO, confirm runner's transcript shape).

TRA-6 Define canonical TRACE task schema and task-validity rubric

## Context TRACE cannot evaluate agents consistently until every task uses the same stable structure and validity standard. This issue defines the shared object consumed by the runner, environment, verifier, attribution system, failure bundle generator, regression tests, and dashboard. ## Objective Define the canonical `TaskSpec` and a task-validity rubric for Month 1 refund/support-agent tasks. ## Scope Include fields for task ID, workflow type, user prompt, available tools, available documents, initial environment state, expected behavior, forbidden actions, required evidence, verifier specification, targeted failure modes, severity, and metadata/version. ## Deliverables * Pydantic model or JSON Schema proposal for `TaskSpec`. * JSON Schema export or equivalent typed contract. * One valid refund/support example. * One invalid or ambiguous example. * Task-validity checklist. * Short rationale for each field. ## Acceptance Criteria * Rupert confirms the schema is runnable by the target-agent runner. * Karan confirms deterministic verifier requirements can be expressed. * Samrath confirms the schema can be serialized, stored, and referenced in traces. * Katharine, if available, confirms the validity rubric is auditable; otherwise mark this review as pending in the issue. * No field relies on undocumented assumptions. ## Suggested Technical Approach Use Python, Pydantic, JSON Schema export, and small fixture files checked into the repository. Keep the schema generalizable beyond the first refund task. ## Collaborators / Reviewers Rupert Maiti, Karan Gupta, Samrath, Evan He, Katharine. ## Dependencies None. This is a Week 1 foundation issue. ## Handoff / Blocks Blocks first task suite, runner integration, verifier implementation, and regression artifacts. ## Out of Scope Do not implement the runner, verifier, environment, or dashboard in this issue.

message=(
                f"deprecated doc(s) {cited} were cited as the basis for actions in a "
                "run that violated current policy"
            ),
            expected=(
                "decisions cite only current-status policy docs; deprecated docs may "
                "be read but never relied on"
            ),
            actual=f"deprecated doc id(s) {cited} appear in reasoning/tool arguments "
            f"at steps {steps}",
            step_ids=steps,
            evidence=[
                EvidenceItem(
                    kind="provenance_quote",
                    description=f"{source} at step {step} cites {doc_id}",
                    step_ids=[step] if step is not None else [],
                    data={"text": text[:500], "doc_id": doc_id},
                )
                for (step, source, text, doc_id) in mentions
            ],
            severity=Severity.HIGH,
            # Diagnosis-grade: the refund/ticket checks already block release;
            # this check explains the source error without double-blocking.
            blocks_release=False,
        )

    def _check_final_answer_consistency(
        self,
        state: SupportState,
        trace: list[TraceEvent],
        warnings: list[str],
    ) -> FailedCheck | None:
        final_events = [e for e in trace if e.event_type is TraceEventType.FINAL_ANSWER]
        if not final_events:
            warnings.append("no final_answer event in trace; consistency check skipped")
            return None
        final_event = final_events[-1]
        answer = str(final_event.payload.get("final_answer", ""))
        lower = answer.lower()
        step_ids = [final_event.step_id] if final_event.step_id is not None else []

        mentions_refund = "refund" in lower or "store credit" in lower
        claims_issued = mentions_refund and any(w in lower for w in _REFUND_ISSUED_WORDS)
        denies_refund = any(p in lower for p in _REFUND_DENIAL_PHRASES)
        refunds_exist = bool(state.refunds)

        # A denial alongside claim-words ("no refund has been issued", "can't
        # issue cash but processed store credit") is not a phantom claim —
        # requiring the absence of the opposite signal keeps correct denials
        # and truthful mixed answers from being flagged.
        if claims_issued and not denies_refund and not refunds_exist:
            return FailedCheck(
                check_id="final_answer_inconsistent_with_state",
                message="final answer claims a refund was issued, but no refund exists in state",
                expected="final answer reflects actual tool side effects",
                actual=f"answer claims an issued refund; state.refunds is empty: {answer[:300]!r}",
                step_ids=step_ids,
                evidence=[
                    EvidenceItem(
                        kind="final_answer",
                        description="final answer text",
                        step_ids=step_ids,
                        data={"final_answer": answer},
                    )
                ],
                severity=Severity.HIGH,
                blocks_release=True,
            )
        if refunds_exist and denies_refund and not claims_issued:
            return FailedCheck(
                check_id="final_answer_inconsistent_with_state",
                message="final answer denies a refund, but a refund was actually issued",
                expected="final answer reflects actual tool side effects",
                actual=(
                    f"state contains {len(state.refunds)} refund(s); answer reads as a "
                    f"denial: {answer[:300]!r}"
                ),
                step_ids=step_ids,
                evidence=[
                    EvidenceItem(
                        kind="final_answer",
                        description="final answer text",
                        step_ids=step_ids,
                        data={"final_answer": answer},
                    ),
                    EvidenceItem(
                        kind="refund_record",
                        description="refunds present in final state",
                        data={"refunds": [r.model_dump(mode="json") for r in state.refunds]},
                    ),
                ],
                severity=Severity.HIGH,
                blocks_release=True,
            )
        if refunds_exist and not claims_issued and not denies_refund:
            warnings.append(
                "a refund exists in state but the final answer does not clearly "
                "mention it; keyword heuristic could not classify the answer"
            )
        return None

reviewComment: `_next_seq` returns `max(existing)+1`, not the “smallest sequence number” that avoids collisions (e.g. existing `REF-0001` and `REF-0003` would yield 4, not 2). Update the docstring to match the actual behavior so callers don’t assume gap-filling.
rejectedCode:
def _next_seq(prefix: str, existing_ids: Iterable[str]) -> int:
    """Smallest sequence number that won't collide with ``<prefix>-NNNN`` ids."""
acceptedCode:
from collections.abc import Iterable
def _next_seq(prefix: str, existing_ids: Iterable[str]) -> int:
    """Smallest sequence number that won't collide with ``<prefix>-NNNN`` ids."""
    pattern = re.compile(rf"{prefix}-(\d+)$")
    taken = [int(m.group(1)) for i in existing_ids if (m := pattern.match(i))]
    return max(taken, default=0) + 1
    # ge=0 bounds: a sign typo in a fixture must fail loudly, not silently

codeContext.reviewedContext:
def _next_seq(prefix: str, existing_ids: Iterable[str]) -> int:
    """Smallest sequence number that won't collide with ``<prefix>-NNNN`` ids."""
    pattern = re.compile(rf"{prefix}-(\d+)$")
    taken = [int(m.group(1)) for i in existing_ids if (m := pattern.match(i))]
    return max(taken, default=0) + 1
codeContext.acceptedContext:
Design notes
    - ``purchase_age_days`` is stored directly instead of purchase dates so
      fixtures stay deterministic (no clock reads anywhere in a run).
    - Side-effect records carry ``*_at_step`` provenance so verifier evidence
      can point at exact trace steps.
    - ``Doc.status`` is load-bearing: the refund failure scenario hinges on
      the agent ignoring ``deprecated`` vs ``current`` metadata.

What does NOT belong here
    Policy *judgment* (that is verifier territory) and retrieval scoring
    (``retrieval.py``). State is dumb data plus lookups.

reviewComment:
## Pull request overview This PR hardens the trace harness pipeline (runner → artifacts → verifier → attribution/bundling → CLI) based on max-effort review findings, aiming to make failures more diagnosable, gating more reliable in CI, and fixture/state/schema handling stricter and less error-prone. **Changes:** - Verifier robustness: improve outage-claim detection scope, policy-doc tie-break selection, and final-answer/state consistency logic; merge verifier results now honors each verifier’s explicit verdict and preserves per-verifier metadata. - Runner/trace/artifacts hardening: AgentRunner is single-use; crash/error events retain step id; final bookkeeping is guarded so `run_result.json` survives snapshot/trace failures; truncated final trace lines are dropped on read. - CLI/config/state/repair artifacts: CLI distinguishes input errors (exit 2) from CI gate failures (exit 1), resolves bare run IDs under `--runs-dir`, and gates incomplete runs; dotenv parsing handles common syntax; state models are stricter (`extra="forbid"`, value bounds, non-colliding seeded IDs); bundling/regression artifacts dedupe repeated check ids and add a grounding control. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 20 out of 20 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | tests/test_refund_verifier.py | Adds verifier regression tests for denial/mixed answers, outage negation scoping, policy tie-break, and merge behavior. | | tests/test_fixture_run.py | Adds runner/state hardening regression tests (runner reuse, pydantic forbid/bounds, ID seeding, snapshot failure resilience). | | tests/test_failure_bundle.py | Tests new grounding control and check-id dedupe behavior in repair packages. | | tests/test_config.py | Adds tests for dotenv parsing and empty env fallback behavior. | | tests/test_cli.py | Adds CLI regression tests for input error exit codes, incomplete-run gating, and bare run-id resolution under `--runs-dir`. | | tests/test_attribution.py | Adds regression tests preventing fabricated missed-recovery narratives and act-then-check recovery windows. | | tests/test_artifact_store.py | Updates trace read tests: truncated tail is dropped; mid-trace corruption still raises with location. | | src/trace_harness/verifiers/refund_policy.py | Refines outage-claim detection, policy doc selection tie-break, and final-answer/state consistency checks. | | src/trace_harness/verifiers/base.py | Fixes merge logic to honor per-verifier `passed` and preserve per-verifier metadata. | | src/trace_harness/tracing/recorder.py | Drops malformed final JSONL line with warning; still raises on earlier corruption. | | src/trace_harness/runner/agent_runner.py | Enforces single-run usage; preserves step id on crash events; guards final snapshot/bookkeeping to protect `run_result.json`. | | src/trace_harness/regression/materializer.py | Dedupes failed check IDs while preserving order in regression artifacts. | | src/trace_harness/failure_bundles/generator.py | Adds grounding control for final-answer consistency and dedupes check IDs for controls/metadata. | | src/trace_harness/environment/state.py | Tightens pydantic models (`extra="forbid"`, bounds), seeds ID counters past existing records to avoid collisions. | | src/trace_harness/config.py | Improves dotenv parsing and ensures empty env values fall back to defaults. | | src/trace_harness/cli.py | Adds `CliInputError`, resolves run dirs under `--runs-dir`, gates incomplete runs, and separates exit codes cleanly. | | src/trace_harness/attribution/heuristic.py | Prevents missed-recovery attribution unless authorization failed; avoids false recovery windows when evidence arrives after harm. | | fixtures/README.md | Reframes fixtures README as a starting-point strawman; removes per-subfolder “owner names” and softens invariant language. | | docs/team_ownership.md | Reframes ownership as stewardship/first reviewer rather than territorial gatekeeping. | | docs/modules.md | Adds strawman notice and clearly separates repo-wide constraints from defaults. |
--- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

Notes: Focus on positioning and product implications, not only paper summary.
